frameworks.name: Three-Lever Scaling Sequence
frameworks.definition: Fix sales motion first, then raise prices, then use increased profit to improve talent retention — each lever unlocks the next.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that for service businesses with a demand constraint (not a supply constraint), the correct sequencing is: fix the sales motion first, increase prices second, and use the resulting profit increase to raise team compensation third — because attempting to raise prices before fixing the sales process will collapse an already-low close rate into an unworkable range.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Price increases before sales-process repair reduce close rate; low close rate produces insufficient profit to fund the compensation improvements needed for retention.
bounded_by: Does not apply when business is supply-constrained (capacity is the binding limit)

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that contacting PPC inbound leads by phone within 60 seconds (rather than by email after sign-up) can roughly double the close rate on those leads — from approximately 30% to approximately 60% — without changing the offer, pricing, or any other element of the sales process.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: price visible on website before sign-up
claims.mechanism: PPC inbound leads have already reviewed the price and self-selected; phone contact before intent fades captures the decision momentum while the buyer is still in buying mode.

numbers.axis: inbound PPC lead close rate by contact method

bounded_by: Applies specifically to PPC inbound where price is pre-disclosed; may not hold for cold outbound or no-price-page funnels

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a pool service business spending $150 to acquire a customer who generates $2,250 annual gross profit (50% gross margin on $4,500/year contract) has a 15-to-1 gross-profit-to-CAC ratio, making additional ad spend economically justified at essentially any scale relative to cash on hand.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: At 15:1 gross-profit-to-CAC, every dollar deployed into ads returns $15 of gross profit over the customer lifetime, making spend expansion the highest-return use of available capital.

numbers.axis: gross profit per dollar of customer acquisition cost

bounded_by: Assumes 93% annual retention holds as volume scales
Does not account for supply constraint — model breaks if fulfillment cannot absorb new demand

claims.text: Alex Hormozi identifies a pattern he calls playing business on defense rather than offense: owners in strong unit-economics positions spend only enough to cover fixed costs but never enough to compound growth, because they are psychologically oriented toward avoiding poverty rather than building wealth.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: playing business on defense rather than offense
conditions: owner is taking salary out of the business
claims.mechanism: Poverty-avoidance psychology caps spend at break-even; the owner allocates capital to satisfy creditors (landlord, bank, team) before themselves, producing a perpetual no-accumulation equilibrium.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that when a service business is in near-equilibrium between supply and demand — enough demand to stretch capacity but not enough to clearly exceed it — the correct move is to acquire the demand first, collect the cash, and then use that cash to fund the additional supply (staff, training) needed to fulfill it.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: get the demand, get the cash, then get the resources
conditions: can collect meaningful revenue in first 30 days of a new customer relationship
staff have flex capacity (can do more hours for higher pay)
claims.mechanism: First-30-day cash collection funds incremental staff costs; because the business collects more in month 1 than it paid to acquire the customer, demand-first scaling is self-financing.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the business has no first-30-day revenue (deferred-payment models)
Does not apply when training a new employee takes 6+ months and the cost of errors is unacceptable

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a pool service business collecting approximately $1,500 from a new customer in the first 30 days (from repairs and initial visits) against a $150 customer acquisition cost is cash-flow-positive on acquisition from day one, eliminating the need for working capital to fund growth.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: you don't even need the money
conditions: first visit includes diagnostic and repair work
$1,500 average first-30-day collection per new customer
roughly 30% gross margin on that first-month revenue
claims.mechanism: When 30-day collection ($1,500) exceeds CAC ($150) by 10x, the business can fund its own growth loop without external capital: each new customer pays for the next acquisition before needing to be retained.

numbers.axis: first-30-day gross profit vs. customer acquisition cost

bounded_by: Assumes first-month revenue includes billable repair work; pure maintenance-only businesses may have lower first-month collection

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that employee retention problems in skilled trade service businesses are proximate causes masking the root cause of under-pricing: employees leave for trades perceived as higher-opportunity (HVAC, plumbing, electrical) because compensation cannot be raised to match without first raising prices.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: employees can exit to adjacent higher-paid trades
business is not charging market-clearing price
claims.mechanism: The chain is: broken sales process prevents price increase -> low prices cap labor budget -> capped labor budget produces below-market wages -> below-market wages drive turnover to higher-paying trades.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that incentivizing a trainer with a time-reduction bonus (e.g., $500 for cutting training time from 6 weeks to 2 weeks) while requiring the trainer to certify the trainee's work quality for 30 days post-graduation aligns the trainer's financial incentive with both speed and quality simultaneously.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: incentivize the trainer to get them on the road faster
conditions: trainer is an experienced employee paid on labor revenue commission
errors by trainee require trainer callback visits
claims.mechanism: Speed bonus creates incentive to compress training time; liability for trainee errors (trainer must fix mistakes for free within the certification window) prevents the trainer from rushing at the expense of quality.
bounded_by: Requires clear, objective quality standard that determines whether a callback is the trainer's responsibility vs. normal service issue

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that increasing monthly ad spend from $500 to $5,000 for the pool service business (a 10x increase) would scale inbound leads from 2-3 per week to approximately 2-3 per day — a roughly 7x increase in lead volume — materially changing the business's growth trajectory.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: go from $500 a month to $5,000 a month to spend
conditions: local PPC (Google Local Services Ads) for pool service in a metropolitan market

numbers.axis: monthly ad spend vs. weekly inbound lead volume

bounded_by: Assumes local market is not saturated at $5,000/month spend level
Assumes fulfillment capacity can be expanded to match demand

examples.text: Phil runs a pool service and repair company in Raleigh, NC at $1.2M annual revenue with 176 recurring customers at ~$4,500/year each (93% annual retention). The company spends $500/month on Google PPC, generating 10-12 inbound leads/month at $50/lead, converting 30% to customers at $150 CAC. Gross margin is 50%, making each customer worth $2,250/year in gross profit. The business collects approximately $1,500 from new customers in the first 30 days (including repair work). Despite these strong unit economics, the owners were not reinvesting into ad spend, citing cash-flow concerns — despite collecting more in the first 30 days than they spent to acquire the customer. Alex Hormozi identified the pattern as playing defense (spending enough to cover costs but not enough to compound growth) and prescribed increasing ad spend 10x as the primary lever.

Phil's pool service company was contacting inbound leads by automated email after sign-up, with no phone call follow-up. Alex Hormozi noted that for PPC inbound leads where the price is already visible on the website, phone contact within 60 seconds is a conversion-doubling lever — from 30% to approximately 60% — achievable without changing the offer or pricing at all. The revenue impact of this one change would effectively equal or exceed another $1.2M in annual revenue from the same lead flow.

Alex Hormozi described gyms from his Gym Launch experience as a parallel to Phil's pool service situation: gym owners with strong unit economics (10:1 return on ad spend) would spend just enough on advertising to cover their fixed costs (rent, equipment leases) but never pushed into profitable territory. They played business on defense — spending enough to avoid insolvency but not enough to build wealth — even though the math clearly justified aggressive scaling. The gym analogy illustrates how poverty-avoidance psychology overrides rational capital allocation even when the investment thesis is obvious.

practices.text: When near supply-demand equilibrium in a service business, prioritize acquiring demand over perfecting supply capacity; use the cash collected in the first 30 days from new customers to fund incremental staffing costs.
Offer employees who are leaving for higher-paying trades the opportunity to earn more money through longer hours and additional jobs, before assuming the only solution is a base pay increase.

When creating a trainer certification program, pair a speed bonus (paid on successful early completion) with a quality liability window (trainer must fix trainee errors for free in the first 30 days post-graduation) to align incentives on both dimensions.

Before diagnosing a retention problem as a compensation problem, check whether the compensation constraint is actually a pricing problem in disguise — the correct sequence is: fix sales process, raise prices, then raise compensation.
